message: The contract specifies caller-level behavior only for the reviewer path (record as skipped_lens and continue the round). For the other in-scope attempt types — executor, review fixer, clarifier, contract drafter, contract reviewer — it requires the cap to fire and the distinct outcome to propagate through processResult, but no acceptance criterion defines the run-level consequence when these are capped (e.g., fail the run loud, abort the stage). This leaves the end-to-end behavior of ~5 of the 6 protected paths unspecified and not directly gatable.

The contract does not state whether the wall-clock cap interacts with a result that arrives just before the hard kill. The idle path has explicit completed_despite_timeout semantics (preserved in scope-out), but the wall-clock path's handling of output/a final result emitted right before the cap-triggered kill is unspecified (preserved vs. discarded). This leaves the boundary between 'wall-clock capped' and 'normal completion' under-defined for the edge case.

The override mechanism's location is ambiguous. The idle override is the --timeout flag, but the floor-enforcement acceptance criterion assumes a 'configuration load' path for rejecting/clamping zero-or-negative values, while scope-in says 'an explicit override path.' The concrete mechanism (flag vs. config key vs. env) is intentionally left open, but the floor criterion's wording may not match a flag-based override, leaving that verification surface slightly under-pinned.

Acceptance criterion 'If every reviewer lens in a round fails or is capped, the existing all-failed hard-error behavior remains loud' has no corresponding fixture in the Tests section, while the sibling partial case (one lens capped, round still converges) does. The outcome is machine-checkable (assert an error is returned when all lenses are capped), but no test is specified to lock it. Add an all-capped/all-failed helper-test case so the loud-failure criterion is backed by a runnable check rather than prose.

Acceptance criterion 'Human and JSON result surfaces distinguish wall-clock cap from generic ACP context cancellation or transport error' is half machine-checkable. The JSON surface is well-covered by the wall_clock_timeout reason, but the 'Human ... result surfaces distinguish' part names no test and no asserted token/string, leaving it as eyeball-verifiable prose. Either specify the distinct token the human output must contain (so a test can assert it) or scope the criterion to the JSON surface.

Validation command set is redundant: `go test ./internal/agents ./internal/app` is a strict subset of `go test ./...`, and `make check` re-runs `go test ./...` again (alongside vet/deadcode). The gate runs fine, so this does not affect soundness or executability — purely a runtime-efficiency note. The narrow package run is worth keeping as a fast first signal during development; `make check` is the comprehensive gate.

scope.in 1 describes 'an explicit override path' for the wall-clock cap but does not state that the override must be bounded. Read alone, scope.in would permit a zero/negative override that silently disables the absolute ceiling — directly contradicting the goal's core invariant ('an agent attempt can never hang indefinitely'). The acceptance criteria do require a positive non-zero floor (reject or clamp), so the overall contract stays coherent and gatable; recommend tightening scope.in to name the override floor so scope.in is self-consistent with the goal.

Load-bearing implicit assumption is unstated: the wall-clock cap must exceed the RESOLVED idle timeout, not just the 25m idle DEFAULT. The idle timeout stays overridable via --timeout, so an operator can legitimately set idle > the 2h wall-clock default; then the wall-clock timer preempts the idle timer and an attempt that truly went idle is recorded as wall_clock_timeout. This silently violates the slice's central distinctness goal and the 'idle still records idle, not wall_clock_timeout' acceptance criterion under a valid config that the default-config tests won't exercise. State the wall_clock_cap > resolved_idle_timeout invariant and what the executor should do when it is violated (clamp / reject / document).

Implicit assumption about cap granularity vs. the reviewer panel is unstated. The cap is per-attempt, but the motivating incident was five concurrent reviewer attempts hung ~5h. The contract silently assumes lenses run concurrently (so a per-attempt cap bounds the round) rather than sequentially (where N lenses x cap is still a multi-hour round-level hang). Make explicit whether the cap bounds an attempt or a round.

Two timer sources (idle watchdog + new wall-clock timer) now cancel the same run context, but the contract leaves implicit (a) that the cancel->killProcessGroup path is idempotent/race-safe when both fire and (b) the precedence rule for which reason is recorded at the boundary. This affects executor behaviour and the idle-vs-wall-clock distinctness the slice depends on; make the precedence/race assumption explicit.

The contract requires a wall-clock cap override but does not explicitly define which real configuration surface must expose it, making the override acceptance criteria potentially satisfiable with only an internal or test-only path.

The contract should explicitly state the assumption that the existing context cancellation / kill path can carry or preserve a distinct wall-clock timeout cause, including when cancellation races with idle timeout or process exit. Otherwise implementers may reuse the same cancel path and lose the ability to reliably distinguish wall_clock_timeout from idle timeout or generic cancellation.
